narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 55.4% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction draws from your overall profile, including your Year 1 school decile of 3—lower than the typical student's 5.71—your Year 3 decile of 2, also below the average of 5.53, a middle family income bracket similar to many applicants, a solid LSAT score of 37 just above the 36.7 average, an impressive undergraduate GPA of 3.8 well exceeding the 3.21 norm, your background as a Hispanic female planning full-time studies, which aligns with common paths though underrepresented in some groups. While your strong undergrad performance and LSAT stand out positively compared to peers, the model weighed other elements more heavily. The biggest influences pushing toward this failure prediction were your lower Year 1 and Year 3 deciles, which had the strongest impact in signaling potential struggles ahead. Following closely were your LSAT score, family income, and racial background, each contributing somewhat to the concern about bar passage. Your high UGPA, gender, and full-time status played smaller roles, still nudging the prediction toward risk but far less decisively. In essence, your early academic deciles carried the most weight, ranking ahead of everything else, unlike many admitted students whose consistent higher deciles tip the scales favorably. Compared to typical applicants, your profile shines in GPA and LSAT but lags in those school rankings, creating the overall close-call prediction. To boost your future bar exam odds, focus on building stronger late-stage academic habits now, like excelling in any remaining coursework to offset those early deciles. Consider targeted LSAT refreshers or bar prep courses tailored for underrepresented students, as even small gains there could shift perceptions. Your full-time commitment is a plus—lean into it with dedicated study routines that mirror successful peers. With your standout GPA as a foundation, proactive steps like these could realistically turn the prediction in your favor next time. Keep pushing; many overcome similar hurdles.
